program.name: KA3 Youth Together ÔÇö European Youth Together 2026

eligibility.additional_rules: Associated partners are allowed but do not count towards minimum eligibility criteria for consortium composition.
eligibility.writing_style:
Formal EU institutional tone, accessible but professional. Write in third person when describing the project ("The project aims to...") and first person plural when describing the consortium ("The
  partnership will...").

  Sentence structure: vary between short declarative sentences and longer compound sentences. Avoid bullet-point lists in narrative sections — use flowing paragraphs with clear topic sentences.

  Vocabulary: use Erasmus+ terminology naturally (e.g., "non-formal learning", "key competences", "European added value", "transnational cooperation", "multiplier effect"). Avoid jargon from other funding
  programmes.

  Tone: confident but not arrogant. Evidence-based, referencing data, studies or prior project experience. Show awareness of EU policy frameworks (EU Youth Strategy 2019-2027, European Pillar of Social Rights,
   Inclusion & Diversity Strategy).

  Avoid: marketing language, vague claims ("innovative" without explanation), overly academic writing, repetition of the same points across sections. Each section should add new information.

  Structure each section with: (1) clear opening statement addressing the evaluation criterion, (2) supporting evidence and specifics, (3) connection to the project design and partnership.
eligibility.ai_detection_rules:
MANDATORY — All generated text must pass as human-written:

  1. SENTENCE VARIATION: Mix short sentences (8-12 words) with medium (15-25) and occasional long (30+). Never start 3+ consecutive sentences with the same word or structure.

  2. NATURAL IMPERFECTIONS: Use occasional subordinate clauses, parenthetical remarks, and varied connectors. Avoid perfectly balanced parallel structures in every paragraph.

  3. SPECIFICITY OVER GENERALITY: Always reference the actual project data — partner names, city names, WP titles, activity types, specific numbers. Generic statements flag AI detection.

  4. VOCABULARY VARIATION: Do not repeat the same adjective/adverb more than twice in 500 words. Rotate synonyms naturally (e.g., "aims/seeks/intends", "addresses/tackles/responds to").

  5. AVOID AI PATTERNS: Never use "it is important to note that", "in conclusion", "furthermore" at paragraph starts repeatedly. Avoid numbered lists unless the form explicitly asks for them.

  6. CONNECTOR DIVERSITY: Rotate transitions — avoid overusing "Moreover", "Furthermore", "Additionally". Use instead: "Building on this", "In parallel", "A key dimension is", "This connects to", "Equally
  relevant".

  7. HUMAN TOUCHES: Include occasional references to practical constraints ("Given the 24-month timeline..."), lessons learned ("Drawing on the coordinator's experience in..."), and realistic challenges ("A
  potential risk is... which will be mitigated by...").

  8. PARAGRAPH LENGTH: Vary between 3-7 sentences per paragraph. Never write paragraphs of exactly the same length consecutively.

sections.title: 1. Relevance of the project
sections.eval_notes:
RELEVANCE OF THE PROJECT (maximum score 30 points)

• Purpose and EU added value: the proposal establishes and develops an EU added-value project that supports policies at EU level relevant for youth – most notably the EU Youth Strategy 2019-2027, e.g. supporting the creation/implementation of policies, policy discussion, and collaboration with youth stakeholders in line with the EU Youth Strategy 2019-2027, the legacy of the European Year of Youth 2022 and the European Youth Work Agenda.

• EU Values: The proposal is relevant for the respect and promotion of shared EU values, such as respect for human dignity, freedom, democracy, equality, the rule of law and respect for human rights, as well as fighting any sort of discrimination.

• Objectives: the proposal objectives are relevant to at least one of the general objectives of the action and at least one of its specific objectives; moreover, the proposal objectives are specific and clearly defined, achievable, measurable, realistic and timely; they address issues relevant to the participating organisations and of a clear added value to the chosen target groups.

• Needs: the proposal demonstrates that it is based on a thorough needs assessment based as far as possible on verifiable facts and figures supported by general and specific data relevant to all countries and organisations in the consortium. A clear needs analysis linking to the concrete realities of applicants, partners and target groups is expected.

• Youth engagement: the proposal demonstrates an active engagement of the partnership with a diverse youth population including those from remote/rural areas and/or with fewer opportunities.

sections.title: 2.2 Quality of the partnership and cooperation
sections.eval_notes:
QUALITY OF THE PARTNERSHIP AND THE COOPERATION ARRANGEMENTS (maximum score 20 points)

• Partnership profile: the partnership involves an appropriate mix of complementary organisations with the necessary profiles, skills, experience, expertise and management support to achieve its objectives; the added value of the for-profit organisations is clearly demonstrated, if involved in the consortium.

• Geographic spread: the partnership demonstrates capacity to reflect the European economic, social and/or cultural diversity through its geographic spread so as to ensure a truly pan European cooperation.

• Local NGOs development: the partnership has the ability to develop the capacities and knowledge of local NGOs that are not already well established at European level to achieve enhanced peer-to-peer collaboration between NGOs across Europe.

• Commitment & tasks: the distribution of responsibilities and tasks in the partnership is clear and appropriate; the coordinator shows high quality management and potential for coordination of transnational networks and leadership in complex environments; young people are suitably involved in all stages of the project implementation.

• Cooperation arrangements: the governance mechanisms proposed will ensure an effective coordination, decision-making, communication and conflict resolution between the participating organisations, participants and any other relevant stakeholders.

sections.title: 3. Impact, dissemination and sustainability
sections.eval_notes:
IMPACT (maximum score 20 points)

• Impact & Sustainability: The proposal identifies pathways for contributing to at least one of the expected impact areas of the action. The steps towards the achievement of the expected impact(s) of the project are clearly identified, logical and credible. Moreover, the project outcomes will have positive and tangible impact on participants and partner organisations. In particular, the project is likely to contribute towards expanding the grassroots organisations' focus of national, regional or local activities not yet cross border in nature, where activities were scaled up or developed at EU level during and after the project lifetime, as well as on the youth community at large. The proposal identifies how the outcomes of the project could potentially contribute to changes at system level in the youth sector both within the project lifetime and beyond, to enable long lasting cooperation at EU level and/or inspire new EU youth policies and initiatives.

• Communication & Dissemination: the proposal demonstrates capacity to undertake youth outreach and ability to communicate effectively on problems and solutions of the communities they represent to a broader global audience; in particular, the proposal provides a sound plan for the communication and dissemination of results and includes appropriate targets, activities and tasks distribution among partners, relevant timing, tools and channels to ensure that the results and benefits will be spread effectively to policy makers and are accessible to end users within and after the project's lifetime. All measures are proportionate to the scale of the project, and contain concrete actions to be implemented both during and after the end of the project.

sections.title: 4. Work plan, work packages and resources

questions.title: Concept and methodology
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Quality of the project design and implementation.

Outline the approach and methodology behind the project. Explain why they are the most suitable for achieving the project's objectives.

questions.title: Project Summary
questions.description: Provide a brief overview of the project.

questions.title: Background and general objectives
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Relevance.

Describe the background and rationale of the project.

How is the project relevant to the scope of the call? How does the project address the general objectives of the call? What is the project's contribution to the priorities of the call (if applicable)?

questions.title: Ethics
questions.description:
Describe any ethics issues that may arise during the project implementation and the measures to address them.

Address gender mainstreaming and children's rights where applicable.

questions.title: Impact and ambition
questions.description:
Please address each guiding point presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Impact.

Define the expected short, medium and long-term effects of the project. Who are the target groups? How will the target groups benefit concretely from the project and what would change for them?

questions.title: Work plan
questions.description: Provide a brief description of the overall structure of the work plan (list of work packages or graphical presentation (Pert chart or similar)).

questions.title: Consortium set-up
questions.description:
Please address all guiding points presented in the Call document/Programme Guide under the award criterion Quality of the partnership and the cooperation arrangements.

Describe the participants (Beneficiaries, Affiliated Entities, Associated Partners and others, if any) and explain how they will work together to implement the project. How will they bring together the necessary expertise? How will they complement each other?

In what way does each of the participants contribute to the project? Show that each has a valid role and adequate resources to fulfil that role.

questions.title: Work packages, activities, resources and timing
questions.description:
Group your activities into work packages. A work package means a major sub-division of the project.

For each work package, enter an objective (expected outcome) and list the activities, milestones and deliverables that belong to it.

Projects should normally have a minimum of 2 work packages. WP1 should cover the management and coordination activities. WP2 and further WPs should be used for the other project activities. The last WP should be dedicated to Impact and dissemination.

Work packages covering financial support to third parties only allowed if authorised in the Call document/Programme Guide.

Enter each activity/milestone/output/outcome/deliverable only once (under one work package).

The Coordinator remains fully responsible for the coordination tasks, even if they are delegated to someone else. Coordinator tasks cannot be subcontracted.

questions.title: Consortium management and decision-making
questions.description:
Explain the management structures and decision-making mechanisms within the consortium. Describe how decisions will be taken and how regular and effective communication will be ensured. Describe methods to ensure planning and control.

Note: The concept (including organisational structure and decision-making mechanisms) must be adapted to the complexity and scale of the project.

questions.title: Communication, dissemination and visibility
questions.description:
Describe the communication and dissemination activities which are planned in order to promote the activities/results and maximise the impact (to whom, which format, how many, etc.). Clarify how you will reach the target groups, relevant stakeholders, policymakers and the general public and explain the choice of the dissemination channels.

Describe how the visibility of EU funding will be ensured.

questions.title: Needs analysis and specific objectives
questions.description:
Please address the specific conditions/objectives set out in the Call document/Programme Guide, if applicable.

Describe how the objectives of the project are based on a sound needs analysis in line with the specific objectives of the call. What issue/challenge/gap does the project aim to address?

The objectives should be clear, measurable, realistic and achievable within the duration of the project. For each objective, define appropriate indicators for measuring achievement (including a unit of measurement, baseline value and target value).

questions.title: Project management, quality assurance and monitoring and evaluation strategy
questions.description:
Describe the measures foreseen to ensure that the project implementation is of high quality and completed in time.

Describe the methods to ensure good quality, monitoring, planning and control.

Describe the evaluation methods and indicators (quantitative and qualitative) to monitor and verify the outreach and coverage of the activities and results (including unit of measurement, baseline and target values). The indicators proposed to measure progress should be relevant, realistic and measurable.

questions.title: Security
questions.description: Describe any security issues if applicable.

questions.title: Sustainability and continuation
questions.description:
Describe the follow-up of the project after the EU funding ends. How will the project impact be ensured and sustained?

What will need to be done? Which parts of the project should be continued or maintained? How will this be achieved? Which resources will be necessary to continue the project? How will the results be used?

Are there any possible synergies/complementarities with other (EU funded) activities that can build on the project results?

questions.title: Project teams, staff and experts
questions.description:
Describe the project teams and how they will work together to implement the project.

List the staff included in the project budget (budget category A) by function/profile and describe briefly their tasks. Provide CVs of all key actors (if required by the Call document/Programme Guide).

questions.title: Complementarity, innovation and EU added value
questions.description:
Explain how the project builds on the results of past activities carried out in the field, and describe its innovative aspects (if any).

Explain how the activities are complementary to other activities carried out by other organisations (if applicable). Illustrate the trans-national dimension of the project; its impact/interest in the EU area; possibility to use the results in other countries, potential to develop cross-border cooperation among Programme countries and Partner countries, if applicable.

If your proposal is based on the results of one or more previous or ongoing projects, please provide precise references to these projects.

questions.title: Cost effectiveness and financial management
questions.description:
Describe the measures adopted to ensure that the proposed results and objectives will be achieved in the most cost-effective way.

Indicate the arrangements adopted for the financial management of the project and, in particular, how the financial resources will be allocated and managed within the consortium.

Do NOT compare and justify the costs of each work package, but summarize briefly why your budget is cost effective.

questions.title: Risk management
questions.description:
Describe critical risks, uncertainties or difficulties related to the implementation of your project, and your measures/strategy for addressing them.

Indicate for each risk (in the description) the impact and the likelihood that the risk will materialise (high, medium, low), even after taking into account the mitigating measures.

Note: Uncertainties and unexpected events occur in all organisations, even if very well-run. The risk analysis will help you to predict issues that could delay or hinder project activities.

criteria.title: Partner expertise & complementarity
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 3 (Partnership, 20pts). Evaluates partner expertise & complementarity quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 3 (Partnership, 20pts) scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Risk identification completeness
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. Evaluates risk identification completeness quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 2 scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. Evaluates budget-activity alignment quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 2 scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Evidence-based needs
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates whether needs are grounded in concrete data from multiple sources and levels. Strong: triangulated evidence from EU statistics, national studies, and grassroots consultation. Weak: assumed needs without evidence.
criteria.structure: Narrative: the gap that exists. Bullets: 3 evidence sources per need. Narrative: why existing solutions are insufficient.
criteria.relations: Needs must trace to objectives in 1.1 and activities in 4.2. Each need should have a corresponding WP that addresses it.
criteria.rules: At least 3 distinct needs identified with supporting data. Sources must be cited.
criteria.red_flags: Needs assumed without data. Single-country perspective. No gap analysis. Needs that the project design cannot address.

score_rubric.100: Triangulated evidence across partner countries with gap analysis

criteria.title: Staff competence & profiles
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2 (Quality, 30pts). Evaluates staff competence & profiles quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 2 (Quality, 30pts) scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Management structure
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. Clear governance with roles, responsibilities, and decision-making processes. Strong: organigram with named roles per partner. Weak: "the coordinator will manage the project."
criteria.structure: Narrative: governance model. Bullets: key roles with responsibilities. Narrative: how structure ensures efficiency.
criteria.relations: Must align with 2.2.2 consortium management and 4.2 WP leadership assignments.
criteria.rules: Coordinator must retain overall responsibility. No subcontracting of core management.
criteria.red_flags: No clear roles. All management on coordinator. No steering committee or advisory structure.

score_rubric.100: Comprehensive governance with steering committee, WP leaders, advisory board, and escalation procedures

criteria.title: Communication strategy & channels
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 4. Evaluates communication strategy & channels quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 4 scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 4 (Impact, 20pts). Evaluates short-term impact on target groups quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.

criteria.title: Methodological framework
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2 (Quality, 30pts). Evaluates whether the project has a coherent methodological approach that justifies WHY chosen methods are most appropriate. Strong: named methodology with adaptation rationale. Weak: list of activities without theoretical grounding.
criteria.structure: Narrative: methodological approach. Bullets: 3 key methodological components. Narrative: why this approach for this context.
criteria.relations: Must align with objectives in 1.2, activities in 4.2, and consortium expertise in 2.2.1.
criteria.rules: Methodology must be described, not just activities. Must explain WHY this approach.
criteria.red_flags: Activity list instead of methodology. No justification for chosen approach. Methodology not suited to target groups.

score_rubric.100: Comprehensive methodology with theoretical basis, adaptation to context, and innovation

criteria.title: Financial sustainability plan
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 4. Evaluates financial sustainability plan quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.
criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 4 scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Call relevance & priorities
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1 (Relevance, 30pts). Evaluates whether the project directly addresses the specific priorities and scope of the KA3 Youth Together call. Strong: names 2-3 call priorities and shows how each WP connects. Weak: generic reference to "Erasmus+ objectives" without call specifics. Differentiator: direct quotes from Programme Guide linking to project activities.
criteria.structure: Open with narrative connecting project rationale to EU youth policy context. Bullet: list 3 call priorities addressed with specific project responses. Close: synthesis of how priorities interlock.
criteria.relations: Must align with 1.2 objectives, 1.3 EU added value, and 3.1 impact ambitions. Eval Criterion 1 also scored via 1.2 and 1.3.
criteria.rules: Must reference at least 2 specific call priorities from Programme Guide. Generic mentions insufficient.
criteria.red_flags: Copy-paste from call text without project-specific connection. No reference to Programme Guide. Mentioning priorities from wrong call/action type.

score_rubric.50: Names priorities but weak connection to project
score_rubric.100: Comprehensive, with Programme Guide references and WP-level connections

criteria.title: Quality assurance plan
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. Specific QA mechanisms, tools, and timeline. Strong: named QA tools with frequency and responsible persons. Weak: "quality will be ensured."
criteria.structure: Narrative: QA philosophy. Bullets: 3 QA mechanisms with tools and timing. Narrative: how QA feeds into adaptation.
criteria.relations: QA findings feed 2.1.5 risk mitigation. QA reports referenced in 3.1 impact evidence.
criteria.rules: Must include both internal and external quality measures.
criteria.red_flags: No specific QA tools. No timeline for QA activities. QA not budgeted.

score_rubric.75: Comprehensive QA plan with tools and timeline
score_rubric.100: Full QA framework with internal + external evaluation, feedback loops, and adaptive management

criteria.title: Activity design quality
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. Evaluates coherence and quality of planned activities. Strong: activities clearly linked to objectives with clear outputs and participant profiles. Weak: generic activities without specifics.
criteria.structure: Narrative: how activities form a coherent programme. Bullets: key activities per WP with outputs. Narrative: logical sequence and interdependencies.
criteria.relations: Activities must match 4.2 WP descriptions, budget in 2.1.4, and timeline. Outputs feed 3.1 impact.
criteria.rules: Each WP must have clear activities with deliverables. Participant numbers must be realistic.
criteria.red_flags: Activities without outputs. Disconnected activities. Unrealistic scope for duration/budget.

score_rubric.100: Comprehensive activity design with clear inputs-outputs-outcomes chain per WP

criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Specific objectives & indicators
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Each objective must have SMART indicators with unit, baseline, and target. Strong: indicator table with baselines from needs assessment. Weak: objectives without measurable outcomes.
criteria.structure: Narrative: how objectives flow from needs. Bullets/Table: objectives with indicator, unit, baseline, target. Narrative: verification methods.
criteria.relations: Each objective maps to at least one WP in 4.2. Indicators feed 2.1.2 monitoring strategy and 3.1 impact measurement.
criteria.rules: SMART format mandatory. Each objective needs at least one quantitative and one qualitative indicator.
criteria.red_flags: Non-measurable objectives. No baseline data. Unrealistic targets. Objectives not traceable to needs.

score_rubric.100: Full indicator framework with baselines, targets, and measurement methodology

criteria.title: Background & rationale
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates the quality of contextual analysis — whether the project is grounded in real evidence about the problem it addresses. Strong: cites 3+ sources (EU data, national reports, grassroots evidence). Weak: vague claims about "youth challenges in Europe". Differentiator: local-level data from partner countries alongside EU-level statistics.
criteria.structure: Narrative: paint the picture of the problem with real data. Bullets: 3 evidence sources (EU, national, local). Narrative: connect to why this consortium is positioned to respond.
criteria.relations: Feeds into 1.2 needs analysis. Must be consistent with 2.2.1 consortium justification and 3.1 impact claims.
criteria.rules: Evidence must be recent (within 5 years). At least one EU-level and one national-level source required.
criteria.red_flags: No data or statistics. All evidence from one country only. Outdated sources (pre-2020). Claims without attribution.

score_rubric.100: Compelling narrative with EU + national + local data from partner countries

criteria.title: Needs-to-design coherence
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates logical chain: identified need → objective → activity → expected result. Strong: clear mapping table showing how each need is addressed. Weak: disconnected needs and activities.
criteria.structure: Narrative: the intervention logic. Bullets: need-objective-activity mapping for top 3 needs. Narrative: why this approach is the most effective response.
criteria.relations: Core coherence check across 1.1, 1.2, 2.1.1 methodology, 4.2 WP design, and 3.1 impact.
criteria.rules: Every identified need must connect to at least one project activity. No orphan needs or orphan activities.
criteria.red_flags: Needs listed but not addressed in work plan. Activities that do not respond to any identified need. Logical gaps in intervention chain.

score_rubric.100: Complete intervention logic with needs-objectives-activities-results chain

criteria.title: Policy uptake & mainstreaming

criteria.structure: Narrative: context and rationale. Bullets: 3 specific elements with evidence. Narrative: synthesis and cross-references to other sections.
criteria.relations: Cross-reference with related sections. Evaluators check consistency across the full proposal. Eval Criterion 4 scoring depends on coherence.
criteria.rules: Follow Programme Guide requirements. Be specific and evidence-based.
criteria.red_flags: Generic claims without evidence. Inconsistency with other sections. AI-generated feel with buzzwords. Missing specific project data.

criteria.title: Monitoring & evaluation
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. How progress is tracked and outcomes measured. Strong: M&E framework with indicators, tools, and reporting schedule. Weak: no monitoring mentioned.
criteria.structure: Narrative: M&E approach. Bullets: key indicators with measurement tools and frequency. Narrative: how M&E drives project adaptation.
criteria.relations: Indicators must trace back to 1.2 objectives. M&E data feeds 3.1 impact assessment and final reporting.
criteria.rules: Must include both quantitative and qualitative indicators. Pre-post measurement for outcomes.
criteria.red_flags: No indicators. Only output indicators (no outcomes). No baseline measurement. No qualitative dimension.

score_rubric.100: Full M&E framework with Theory of Change, baseline, midterm, final, and follow-up measurement

criteria.structure: Narrative: NFE philosophy. Bullets: 3 specific NFE methods used. Narrative: expected learning outcomes.
criteria.relations: Must be reflected in 2.1.3 staff competencies and 4.2 activity descriptions.
criteria.rules: KA3 Youth requires non-formal learning approaches. Must demonstrate youth participation in design.
criteria.red_flags: Purely formal education methods. No mention of youth participation. No specific NFE tools named.

score_rubric.100: Comprehensive NFE approach with youth co-design, specific tools, and learning outcomes

criteria.title: General objectives clarity
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates whether the project has a clear overarching goal that logically flows from the background analysis. Strong: one main goal with 3 specific sub-goals, each traceable to identified needs. Weak: list of 6+ vague objectives. Differentiator: Rule of Three — exactly 3 well-defined objectives.
criteria.structure: Narrative: state the main goal in one powerful sentence. Bullets: 3 specific objectives with measurable indicators. Narrative: explain how objectives address the identified gap.
criteria.relations: Objectives must map 1:1 to needs in 1.2, activities in 4.2, and expected results in 3.1. Evaluators cross-check this alignment.
criteria.rules: Objectives must be SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound). Each objective needs at least one indicator.
criteria.red_flags: More than 5 objectives (unfocused). No measurable indicators. Objectives disconnected from background. Buzzword objectives without substance.

score_rubric.50: Clear but not SMART, missing indicators
score_rubric.75: SMART objectives with indicators, Rule of Three
score_rubric.100: Perfect SMART objectives with baseline values, targets, and timeline

criteria.title: Role clarity & resource adequacy
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 3 (Partnership, 20pts). Evaluates role clarity & resource adequacy quality and specificity. Strong: concrete evidence with three dimensions. Weak: generic or missing. Differentiator: project-specific detail with cross-references.

criteria.title: EU policy alignment
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates connection to broader EU youth policy frameworks (EU Youth Strategy 2019-2027, European Youth Goals, Inclusion & Diversity Strategy). Strong: names specific EU policy objectives and shows how project contributes. Weak: no mention of EU policy context.
criteria.structure: Narrative: position the project within the EU youth policy landscape. Bullets: 3 specific policy documents/objectives referenced. Narrative: explain the project contribution to these policy goals.
criteria.relations: Supports 1.3 EU added value. Must be consistent with 3.1 impact at European level. References should appear in dissemination strategy 3.2.
criteria.rules: At least 2 EU policy references required. Must be current policy frameworks (post-2019).
criteria.red_flags: No EU policy references. Citing outdated frameworks. Forced connections to irrelevant policies. Listing policies without explaining project contribution.

score_rubric.100: Deep integration with EU Youth Strategy, Youth Goals, and call-specific priorities

criteria.relations: Connects to Inclusion & Diversity Strategy. Must be reflected in 1.1 target groups and 2.1.4 budget allocation.
criteria.rules: Erasmus+ horizontal priority. Must address at least financial, geographic, and social barriers.
criteria.red_flags: No inclusion strategy. Token mention without concrete measures. Budget does not reflect inclusion costs.

score_rubric.100: Embedded inclusion with specific measures, budget, monitoring, and intersectional approach

criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 2. How project addresses digital transformation and environmental sustainability (Erasmus+ horizontal priorities). Strong: specific digital tools and green measures. Weak: no mention.
criteria.structure: Narrative: digital and green integration. Bullets: 3 measures each. Narrative: how these enhance project quality.
criteria.relations: Must be reflected in activities 4.2 and dissemination 3.2. Green measures in travel/event planning.
criteria.rules: Erasmus+ horizontal priorities. Should be addressed even if not main focus.
criteria.red_flags: Complete absence of digital/green. Greenwashing without concrete measures.

score_rubric.100: Digital and green embedded throughout with specific tools, metrics, and OER commitment

criteria.title: Target group identification
criteria.meaning: Maps to Eval Criterion 1. Evaluates whether target groups are clearly defined with three levels: primary beneficiaries, intermediary actors, and structural/institutional targets. Strong: quantified targets per group with geographic distribution. Weak: "young people" without further specification.
criteria.structure: Narrative: describe who benefits and why. Bullets: 3 target group levels with numbers and profiles. Narrative: how selection criteria ensure reaching intended groups.
criteria.relations: Must be consistent across 1.2, 3.1 impact, 3.2 dissemination targets, and 4.2 WP participant numbers. Numbers must match budget in 2.1.4.
criteria.rules: Primary target group must include young people (KA3 Youth requirement). Numbers must be realistic for budget and duration.
criteria.red_flags: No quantification. Only one target group level. Numbers inconsistent with budget. No mention of disadvantaged or underrepresented youth.

score_rubric.100: Three levels, quantified, geographically distributed, with selection criteria and inclusion strategy
